coredata.dc:title: Morphology-engineered carbon quantum dots embedded on octahedral CdIn2S4 for enhanced photocatalytic activity towards pollutant degradation and hydrogen evolution
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Qiyuan
Wang, Linghui
Jiang, Yinhua
Liu, Yan
Zhang, Wenli
Zhang, Jianming
Olayemi Macauley, Andre Lennox
coredata.dc:description: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CdIn2S4 have considered as the representatives of the most potential photocatalysts applied in the field of photocatalysis for efficiently solving energy shortage and environmental pollution. In this work, a novel CQDs hybridized CdIn2S4 (CQDs/CIS) heterostructure with 2D nanosheet/3D nanooctahedra morphology was successfully fabricated by a simple in-situ solvothermal method. Most interestingly, the morphology of hybrid gradually evolved from 3D octahedron to 2D nanosheet with the increase of CQDs. This unique 2D/3D structure and synergistic effect between CQDs and CdIn2S4 increased the multi-dimensional active reaction sites and enhanced the quantum yield and the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron pairs. Therefore, CQDs/CIS hybrids showed excellent photocatalytic activities of H2 generation, RhB and TCH degradation. Especially, CQDs/CIS-3 heterostructure presented the highest photocatalytic efficiency and its hydrogen generation activity (956.79 μmol g−1 h−1) was 7.57-fold improvement by contrast with pure CdIn2S4 (126.35 μmol g−1 h−1). Moreover, RhB and TCH degradation rate constants of CQDs/CIS-3 were about 8.14 and 2.32 times higher than those of CdIn2S4, respectively. Furthermore, the effect of CQDs on the evolution of heterostructure morphology and photocatalytic mechanism were also proposed. This research work would offer useful enlightenment for elucidating the affect of CQDs on the morphology evolution and construction of CQDs-based hybrid with excellent performances for H2 production and pollutant removal.

head.ce:title: Morphology-engineered carbon quantum dots embedded on octahedral CdIn2S4 for enhanced photocatalytic activity towards pollutant degradation and hydrogen evolution

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In recent years, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) and CdIn2S4 have considered as the representatives of the most potential photocatalysts applied in the field of photocatalysis for efficiently solving energy shortage and environmental pollution. In this work, a novel CQDs hybridized CdIn2S4 (CQDs/CIS) heterostructure with 2D nanosheet/3D nanooctahedra morphology was successfully fabricated by a simple in-situ solvothermal method. Most interestingly, the morphology of hybrid gradually evolved from 3D octahedron to 2D nanosheet with the increase of CQDs. This unique 2D/3D structure and synergistic effect between CQDs and CdIn2S4 increased the multi-dimensional active reaction sites and enhanced the quantum yield and the separation efficiency of photogenerated electron pairs. Therefore, CQDs/CIS hybrids showed excellent photocatalytic activities of H2 generation, RhB and TCH degradation. Especially, CQDs/CIS-3 heterostructure presented the highest photocatalytic efficiency and its hydrogen generation activity (956.79 μmol g−1 h−1) was 7.57-fold improvement by contrast with pure CdIn2S4 (126.35 μmol g−1 h−1). Moreover, RhB and TCH degradation rate constants of CQDs/CIS-3 were about 8.14 and 2.32 times higher than those of CdIn2S4, respectively. Furthermore, the effect of CQDs on the evolution of heterostructure morphology and photocatalytic mechanism were also proposed. This research work would offer useful enlightenment for elucidating the affect of CQDs on the morphology evolution and construction of CQDs-based hybrid with excellent performances for H2 production and pollutant removal.

ce:section.ce:para: In today's society, the rapid development of economy and people's living standards have put higher requirements for energy and environment, however, energy shortage and environmental pollution are the two main key issues to inhibit human sustainable development (AbdElgawad et al., 2021; Zhou et al., 2021). Therefore, it is particularly critical to develop some advanced technologies to solve the above two challenges simultaneously. In recent years, photocatalytic technology as one of green and efficient mitigation to alleviate the environmental and energy issues has received widespread attention (Zhu et al., 2022), and it has shown promising applications in water splitting for H2 evolution (Chandrappa et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2021), CO2 photoreduction (Nguyen et al., 2020), nitrogen fixation (D. Sun et al., 2021), heavy metal reduction (Li et al., 2021; L. Zhang et al., 2021) and pollutant degradation (Zhu et al., 2020). In photocatalysis system, the selection of photocatalyst and the rational structure design are the two key points to obtain high photocatalytic efficiency (Zhong et al., 2022).

Up to now, a great of attempts have been focused on designing and constructing various semiconductor photocatalysts such as metal oxide (TiO2 (Zhang et al., 2021), ZnO (Majumder et al., 2020), BiOI (Sharma et al., 2021)), sulfides (CdS (Nivetha et al., 2022), ZnIn2S4 (Jiang et al., 2022), CdIn2S4 (Zhao et al., 2021)), nitrides (C3N4 (Iqbal et al., 2022), Ta3N5 (Jouypazadeh et al., 2021)), carbides (Mo2C (Zhao et al., 2020), WC (Ren et al., 2021), Ti3C2 (Sharma et al., 2022)) etc to improve the efficiency of photocatalysis systems. Recently, CdIn2S4, as being a promising sulfide photocatalyst, has been extensively exploited in the photocatalysis (S. Wang et al., 2017), solar cell (T. Wang et al., 2017), photoelectrical application (Dhandole et al., 2019) and so on, due to its high stability (Pei et al., 2021), strong visible-light-responsive ability (Eg ≈ 2.0–2.2 eV) (C. Liu et al., 2019), suitable VB and CB positions (Yendrapati et al., 2021), and superior photoelectrochemical and structural properties (Liang et al., 2020). Nevertheless, further practical application of single-component CdIn2S4 is hampered by its fast charge pair recombination, limited visible light utilization and fewer active sites (Ling et al., 2017). Construction of CdIn2S4 based heterojunctions with different structures has appeared to be one of the most effective strategies to solve its inherent defects (Guo et al., 2021; Jiang et al., 2018; Mahadadalkar et al., 2018). For example, Kale et al. reported a TiO2/CdIn2S4 heterojunction by loading TiO2 nanoparticles on the sheets of CdIn2S4 microspheres with a significant improvement for photocatalytic H2 production (Mahadadalkar et al., 2018). A novel 1D-2D CdIn2S4/CNFs/Co4S3 heterojunction was prepared by Zhang et al. which exhibited a superior photocatalytic H2 production activity and photostability (Guo et al., 2021). Dang et al. constructed a ZnIn2S4/CdIn2S4 nano-composite through modifying ZnIn2S4 nanosheets by loading CdIn2S4 nano-octahedron, presenting excellent photocatalytic H2 generation activity. Our previous work also reported a novel 3D/2D CdIn2S4/ZnO heterostructure with efficient TCH photodegradation (Jiang et al., 2018). These above cases indicated that rational design of the morphology and microstructure of CdIn2S4 based heterostructures could efficiently boost electron transport and photocatalytic properties, however, most of these research were devoted to improve the single photocatalytic activity such as H2 generation or pollutant photodegradation, and very few reports involved the enhancement of multiphotocatalytic activities of CdIn2S4 heterostructure (Bariki et al., 2020; Huang et al., 2020). Thus, seeking a highly active, low-cost and suitable cocatalyst to delicately configure and design CdIn2S4 related heterostructure with the proper architecture for improving its multiphotocatalytic activities to fit for its future practical application is very necessary and challenge.

Recently, by hybridizing semiconductor quantum dots (QDs) to the photocatalytic system is of equal efficient and important for increasing the visible light harvesting and photo-conversion efficiency, thus improving the photocatalytic performance (An et al., 2017; Chen et al., 2021; Yan et al., 2018). Particularly, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are among these superior cophotocatalysts for improving the photocatalytic performance, owing to eco-friendly and cheap (Sharma et al., 2019), superior electron reservoir properties (Dinda et al., 2020), outstanding photo-induced electron transfer (Kaur et al., 2021) and excellent up-converting performance (Zhou et al., 2021). Various of CQDs based heterojunctions such as ZnO/carbon quantum dots (Li et al., 2013), CQDs/TiO2/WO3 (J. Wang et al., 2021), CQDs/BiOBr (Duo et al., 2016), N-CQDs/InNbO4 (Zhao et al., 2021), PANI/CQDs (El-Sawy et al., 2021), CQDs/MgIn2S4/BiOCl (Qi et al., 2022) etc, have been widely and continuously researched. In addition, CQDs have also been utilized to modify heterojunction photocatalysts as a bridge for promoting the charge transfer to further raise their photocatalytic performance. For example, Sun et al fabricated TiO2/WO3/CQDs nanocomposite to enhance cephalexin photocatalytic mineralization, and the adding of CQDs could increase charge separation and light absorption (Sun et al., 2021). Liang et al. synthesized a g-C3N4/CQDs/CdIn2S4 composite to exhibit enhanced ibuprofen photodegradation (Liang et al., 2020). Huang et al. constructed the CQDs@CdIn2S4/CdS composite, and found that the modification of CQDs greatly improved the hydrogen generation and degradation (Huang et al., 2020). Significant improvements of the photocatalytic performance have been reached by applying these CQDs modified heterostructures.

In view of the advantages of CQDs and CdIn2S4 and the inspiration of the above cases, the synthesis strategy of a binary heterostructure of CQDs and CdIn2S4 will also be very efficient in improving of photocatalytic activity of pure CdIn2S4. If CQDs can be successfully embedded into CdIn2S4 structure, the fabricated composite heterostructure will present the broaden visible-light response region, the quantum yield of charge pairs and fast charge carrier separation rate due to the outstanding photo-induced electron transfer and excellent up-converting performance of CQDs. As a result, a highly-efficient CQDs hybridized CdIn2S4 heterostructure system will be obtained. However, this promising heterostructure has not been studied until now, and the influence of CQDs on morphological heterostructure and enhancement of photocatalytic activity are worthy of systematically investigating for its further practical applications.

Herein, we demonstrated the design and synthesis of a novel carbon quantum dots (CQDs) hybridized CdIn2S4 heterostructure (CQDs/CIS) with 3D nanooctahedra/2D nanosheet morphology by an in-situ solvothermal method. The effectiveness of the CQDs amount on the morphology evolution and the photocatalytic activity of the heterostructrue were carefully studied. SEM and TEM characterization found that the adding amount of CQDs had a great influence on the growth morphology of CdIn2S4, and the morphology of CQDs/CIS hybrids gradually evolved from 3D octahedron to 2D nanosheet with the increase of CQDs amount. According to our knowledge, this unique architecture composed of 3D octahedron and 2D nanosheet heterojunctions was first reported. The as-prepared 3D/2D CQDs/CIS composites exhibited much superior photocatalytic activities for H2 generation, RhB and TCH degradation than pure 3D CdIn2S4 octahedron. The enhanced photocatalytic performance was carefully discussed. The photostability and recyclability of CQDs/CIS composite were also evaluated by the cycling experiment. Additionally, the electron-hole transfer and photocatalytic reaction mechanism were proposed in detail.

CQDs solution was prepared referring to a literature followed by the freeze-drying (Raikwar, 2022). The CQDs/CdIn2S4 (CQDs/CIS) hybrid was fabricated through the in-situ solvothermal approach. The detail preparation process of CQDs/CdIn2S4 heterostructure was described as follows: First, 0.4 mmol Cd(NO3)2 .4H2O, 3.2 mmol thioacetamide and 0.8 mmol In(NO3)3 .4.5H2O, were dissolved in 30 mL 50 v% ethanol solution under the stirring until a clear solution was obtained. Then, according to the weight ratio of CQDs and CdIn2S4, a proper amount of CQDs solution was put in above solution followed by the vigorous stirring and ultrasonic treatment for 20 min. After continuously stirring for 2 h, the suspension was transferred into 80 mL Teflon-lined autoclave and then heated to 180 °C for 24 h. Next, the resulting precipitates were collected after the reactor was cooled. Subsequently, deionized water and ethanol were used to wash the obtained samples, and then the samples were dried at 60 °C in air for 24 h. The CQDs/CIS nanostructures with different weight ratios between CQDs and CdIn2S4 were obtained. The as-prepared CQDs/CIS hybrids with the CQDs weight ratio of 1, 3 and 5 wt% were recorded as CQDs/CIS-1, CQDs/CIS-3 and CQDs/CIS-5, respectively. Moreover, pure CdIn2S4 was also synthesized only without adding the CQDs for comparison. The preparation processes of CQDs/CIS hybrids were presented in Scheme 1 .
These contents are listed in the supporting materials.

SEM images of pure CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS heterostructure were shown in Fig. 1 . From Fig. 1a, pure CdIn2S4 particles were in a regular pointed 3D bipyramidal structures with the uniform size, clear boundary, smooth surface and good dispersion. When CQDs were introduced to the CdIn2S4 system, the morphologies of as-prepared composites dramatically changed (Fig.1b-d), which manifested that the addition of CQDs played an important role in the growth process of CdIn2S4. When 1 wt% CQDs was added, the composite still showed relatively regular pointed bipyramidal structure, however, the equatorial boundaries of the junction of two pyramids started to extend outwards and became larger. When 3 wt% CQDs was added, two kinds of shapes of 2D nanosheet and 3D bipyramid in CQDs/CIS-3 composite could be easily found in SEM images (Fig. 1c). The sizes of CQDs/CIS bipyramids obviously became smaller than those in pure CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-1. Interesting, in CQDs/CIS-3 sample, it could be found that a layer of 2D nanosheet CQDs/CIS heterostructure was warped on the equatorial boundaries of each 3D bipyramid CQDs/CIS heterostructure like a little girl in a lacy skirt. It is worth noting that this morphology of heterostructrue was first reported. When the amount ratio of CQDs increased to 5 wt%, the composite presented the partially adhesive mixed morphology of a large number of folded thin 2D nanosheets wrapped with a number of small 3D bipyramids. In addition, the size of 2D nanosheet gradually increased and the size of 3D bipyramids gradually decreased with the increase of CQDs amount. The above results proved that adding of CQDs greatly affected CdIn2S4 crystal growth, and more amount of CQDs would result in the morphology of the heterostructure gradually evolving from 3D bipyramids into 2D nanosheets.

The changes of the morphology and microstructure of the as-prepared CQDs/CIS heterostructure were also easily detected from the TEM images (Fig. 2 c–e). CdIn2S4 sample presented the dispersed dark rhombic or cubic shapes, which were the typical projections of octahedral morphology under TEM observation (Fig. 2a). From Fig. 2b, the monodisperse and nonaggregative CQDs nanoparticles were uniformly distributed, and the particle size was in the range of 2–4 nm presenting the property of quantum dots. The TEM images of CQDs/CIS heterostructures were shown in Fig. 2c–e. Compared with the pure CdIn2S4, the rhombic structure of sample CQDs/CIS-1 (Fig. 2c) showed an obvious change, and it presented the dark inner core and light edges were observed. This chromatic aberration was on behalf of the difference in thickness, which greatly proved that the existing of CQDs influenced the growth of CdIn2S4 crystals. At the same time, the CQDs nanopartilces were wrapped inside of the CdIn2S4 crystal, which confirmed that the hybrids of CQDs and CdIn2S4 had been successfully synthesized. When 3 wt% CQDs was added, TEM image (Fig. 2d) showed that the rhombus structure of the sample gradually changed to an irregular square structure with relatively uniform and light color. Compared with that of CQDs/CIS-1, it was very difficult to distinguish the dark core and the light edge, indicating that morphology evolution gradually from bipyramid to nanosheets. With the continuous increase of CQDs amount, more relatively regular square and thin structure with much more folded edges were observed, indicating that the more nanosheets were formed. The morphology evolution of CQDs/CIS samples with the increase of CQDs was listed in Scheme 2 .

Such morphological evolution can be explicable with the nucleation of primary particles and growth theory (Cheng et al., 2005; Liu et al., 2019). When no CQDs was added, the nucleation process of primary particles was relatively difficult due to the homogeneous nucleation. Only with a long enough reaction time could the crystal nuclei form, and these crystal nuclei eventually self-aggregated via two or three dimensional growth into a bipyramidal structure followed the growth mechanism of Ostwald ripening. When CQDs was added, these CQDs nanoparticles could act as nucleation centers, making the crystallization process much easier, thus forming a large number of crystal nuclei in a very short time. However, under the limited concentration of reactants, nucleation and growth competed with each other, especially when the content of CQDs was higher. The growth mechanism of oriented attachment should dominate compared with Ostwald ripening growth mechanism, and crystal nuclei would share a common crystallographic orientation and joined at the planar interfaces (Luo et al., 2009). Therefore, two dimensional growths would dominate and three-dimensional growth would be somewhat inhibited to some extent due to the large amount of nucleation nanoparticles and limited reactants, thus forcing the crystal nucleus to grow and assemble from 3D bipyramids into 2D nanosheets. At the same time, the OH on the surface of CQDs also should affect the growth direction of CdIn2S4 crystal nanoparticles. It was obvious that more amount of CQDs should be responsible for the formation of 2D nanosheet. However, the detail mechanism for the morphology transfer from 3D bipyramids into 2D nanosheets is still under study, which requests more experiments and simulation.

In order to further verify the hybridization of CQDs and CdIn2S4, HRTEM image of/CIS-3 hybrid material was taken and shown in Fig. 2f. The lattice with fringe spacing was determined to be 0.321 nm, which was corresponding to the with (002) crystal plane of CQDs. In addition, the interlaced lattice spacings of about 0.326, 0.388 and 0.193 nm were related to (311), (220) and (440) planes of cubic CdIn2S4. SAED pattern (inset in Fig. 2f) suggested that the composite was polycrystalline. The elemental mappings (Fig. 2g) revealed the uniform distribution of Cd, In, S and C in the composite, proving the successful hybridization of CQDs and CdIn2S4.

The as-prepared CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-x hybrid materials were investigated by XRD analysis (Fig. 3 ). From Fig. 3a, bulk CdIn2S4 showed the characteristic peaks of cubic phase at 14.2°, 23.3°, 27.3°, 28.6°, 33.0°, 40.8°, 43.3°, 47.4°, 55.6°, 66.3° and 76.0°, corresponding to (111), (220), (311), (222), (400), (422), (511), (440), (533), (731) and (751) lattice planes (JCPDS card No.27–0060J, respectively (Jiang et al., 2018). After modification with CQDs, all CQDs/CIS-x composites only exhibited the characteristic peaks belonging to CdIn2S4 and no impure crystalline phase could be detected, implying that the modification of CQDs hardly influenced the crystal structure of CdIn2S4. Moreover, no peaks of CQDs could be observed due to the low content and small particle size of CQDs in the samples. The enlarged XRD patterns in the range of 25–30° were further used to study the interaction between of CQDs and CdIn2S4 (Fig. 3b). From Fig. 3b, it was clearly found that the diffraction peaks at 27.3° related to CdIn2S4 all slightly shifted to lower diffraction angles with the increase of CQDs loading content, which well indicated there existed the interaction between CQDs and CdIn2S4 for favoring the transfer of photogenerated charge pairs.

The existence of CQDs in the CQDs/CdIn2S4 heterostructure was further judged by FT-IR analysis (Fig. S1). From the spectrum of CQDs, the absorption peak at 3406 cm−1 corresponded to the stretching vibration of O–H from the adsorbed water. The small broad peak concentrated about at about 2940 cm−1 belonged to the C–H stretch of polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons (Xie et al., 2017). Two peaks observed at 1654 and 1560 cm−1 were CO vibrational absorption band and N–H bending vibrations, respectively (Xu et al., 2017). For CdIn2S4 sample, the broad bands at 3436 and 1624 cm−1 were due to O–H stretching vibrations of absorbed water. After CQDs were introduced to the CdIn2S4, FT-IR spectra of CQDs/CIS-x composites were almost the same as that of the CdIn2S4 because of the small amount of the CQDs. However, a small broad peak ranged from 2920 to 2980 cm−1 was related to the C–H of CQDs could be gradually found with the increase of CQDs content, indicating the successful hybridization of CdIn2S4 and CQDs.

The survey XPS spectra of pure CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-3 samples and high resolution spectra of Cd 3d, In 3d, S 2p and C1s were presented in Fig. 4 a–e. The survey scan spectrum of CQDs/CIS-3 indicated the presence of Cd, In, S, C and O peaks. The small O peak at 532.30 eV indicated the presence of absorbed H2O (Fan et al., 2008). Fig. 4b showed Cd 3d spectra in the CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-3. The two main peaks centered at 405.2 and 412.3 eV belonged to Cd 3d3/2 and Cd 3d5/2, respectively, which corresponded to Cd2+ in the CdIn2S4 sample. As discerned from XPS spectra of In 3d (Fig. 4c), two individual peaks appeared at 444.8 and 452.1 eV respectively ascribed to 3d3/2 and 3d5/2 of typical In3+. The deconvoluted S 2p XPS spectrum of pure CdIn2S4 sample (Fig. 4d) presented two peaks at 160.1 and 161.3 eV, which were assigned to S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 in the form of S2−, respectively. Significantly, the binding energies of S 2p3/2 and S 2p1/2 were found to be at 160.7 and 161.9 eV in CQDs/CIS-3, which slightly shifted to higher binding energies implying the strong electronic interaction between CQDs and CdIn2S4 in the composites, which was similar to the reported literature (Xia et al., 2017). The XPS spectrum of C1s for sample CQDs/CIS-3 could be deconvoluted into three peaks at 284, 285.6 and 288 eV (Fig. 4e) assigned to C–C/CC, C–O and CO bonds, respectively, further confirming the presence of CQDs in CQDs/CIS-3 composite (Y. Liu et al., 2019; Niu et al., 2020). The above results fully demonstrated that CQDs had successfully combined with CdIn2S4 nanoparticle and there existed good interfacial contacts between CQDs and CdIn2S4.

The N2 adsorption-desorption isotherms were done to determine BET surface areas and porosity of CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-x samples. Shown in Fig S2, all samples exhibited IV isotherms with the H3 type hysteresis loop, indicating the mesoporous structures. The inset in Fig. S2 was the pore-size distribution curves of CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-x samples, which further confirmed the mesoporous properties. The specific surface area of pure CdIn2S4 was calculated to be 37.5.71 m2/g, whereas SBET of CQDs/CIS-1, CQDs/CIS-3 and CQDs/CIS-5 were 43.52, 81.34 and 98.03 m2 g−1, respectively. Obviously, the hybridization of CQDs and CdIn2S4 greatly increased the specific surface area. The more CQDs was added, the greater the specific surface area would be. The reason could be ascribed to the different morphology of CQDs/CIS-x composites with adding different amounts of CQDs. From the SEM and TEM images, it could be easily found the morphology of composite gradually changed from 3D nanooctahedron to 2D nanosheets, which should increase the specific surface area. Furthermore, the mixed morphology of 3D nanooctahedron and 2D nanosheets could increase the multi-dimensional active reaction sites for further improving photocatalytic performance.

The UV–vis DRS of pure CdIn2S4 and the CQDs/CIS hybrid materials were applied to study the optical adsorption properties and the results were presented in Fig. 5 a and Fig. 5b. From Fig. 5a, the adsorption edge of CdIn2S4 was about 597 nm, indicating its superior visible light adsorption capacity. After CQDs were added, the adsorption edges of composites all res-shifted obviously, which were 611, 624 and 644 nm for sample CQDs/CIS-1, CQDs/CIS-3 and CQDs/CIS-5, respectively. Besides, the optical band gaps (Eg) of pure CdIn2S4, CQDs/CIS-1, CQDs/CIS-3 and CQDs/CIS-5 composites are determined to be 2.07, 2.03, 1.98 and 1.92 eV by extrapolating the linear portion of curves of (ahv)1/2 versus hv, respectively (Fig. 5b). The above DRS results showed that all CQDs/CIS composites could efficiently utilize visible light and presented high quantum yield of photoinduced charge pairs for enhanced photocatalytic performances. Moreover, Mott-Schottky experiment was used to calculate the band potentials of CdIn2S4 (Fig. 5c). From the curve, the positive slope of C−2 –E plot signified CdIn2S4 as n-type semiconductor, and the flat-band potential (EFB) was −0.64 eV (vs. SCE), which could be converted to be about −0.40eV (vs. NHE) (Jiang et al., 2018). For n-type semiconductor, the conduction band potential (ECB) was commonly thought to be 0.1 V lower than its EFB potential (Xiao et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2021). Therefore, the ECB value of CdIn2S4 were calculated to be −0.50 eV. From the result of DRS, it could be known that the Eg of pure CdIn2S4 was 2.07eV. Based on the formula of EVB = ECB + Eg (Dong et al., 2021), the valence band potential of CdIn2S4 (EVB) were determined to be 1.57eV (vs. NHE).

The PL spectrum, transient photocurrent response and EIS measurements were further recorded to study the properties of as-prepared samples (Fig. 6 ). Fig. 6a showed PL emission spectra of pure CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-x nanocomposites under 300 nm excitation. It was evident that the intensities of PL emission peaks for CQDs/CIS-x nanocomposites were obviously lower than that of pure CdIn2S4, which was attributed to the fast electron transfer from CdIn2S4 surface to CQDs, and such spatial separation of the photoinduced charge carriers efficiently reduced their recombination (Liang et al., 2018). PL intensity of CQDs/CIS-x followed the order of CQDs/CIS-1 > CQDs/CIS-5 > CQDs/CIS-3, which indicated that 3D nanooctahedron/2D nanosheet mixed morphology greatly boosted the separation efficiency of photoinduced electron-hole pairs compared with 3D nanooctahedron or 2D nanosheet. And CQDs/CIS-3 had the lowest intensity of PL emission peak, implying that it should possess the optimal photocatalytic performance (Che et al., 2020). The photocurrent-time (I-t) and EIS curves of pure CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-3 were presented in Fig. 6b and 6c. Obviously, the higher photocurrent response and the smaller EIS radius in Nyquist plot meant the more efficient separation and lower recombination rate of photoinduced charges in CQDs/CIS-3 composite. Therefore, it could be believed that CQDs/CIS-3 composite with this novel 3D/2D mixed morphology formed by the hybridization of CQDs would be favorable for the enhancement photocatalytic performance.

For evaluating the photocatalytic performance of CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-x heterostructures, the simulated solar light water splitting for H2 evolution reaction was performed (Fig. 7 ). As shown in Fig. 7a, pure CdIn2S4 showed very low hydrogen evolution capacity of about 631.72 μmol g−1 for 5 h owing to the its high recombination of photogenerated charge pairs. After the adding the carbon CQDs for the in-situ growth of CdIn2S4, all the CQDs/CIS-x heterostructures obviously possessed higher H2 generation abilities. H2 evolution amounts of CQDs/CIS-1, CQDs/CIS-3 and CQDs/CIS-5 samples were about 1981.00, 4783.96 and 3404.35 μmol g−1, respectively. The average rates of H2 generation are displayed in Fig. 7b. Obviously, the rates of the H2 evolution for all CQDs/CIS-x hybrids dramatically increased and the adding amount of CQDs played an important part in the enhancement of photocatlytic performance. CQDs/CIS-3 sample obtained the optimal photocatalytic activity for Hydrogen evolution. H2 generation rate reached to 956.79 μmol g−1 h−1, which showed a 7.57-fold improvement by contrast with pure CdIn2S4 (126.35 μmol g−1 h−1). From the photocatalytic H2 generation experiment result, it could be concluded that CQDs modification was really a good strategy for efficiently improving the photocatalytic activity. In addition, the formation of 3D nanooctahedra/2D nanosheet mixed morphology of CQDs/CIS hybrid could dramatically increase of the photocatalytic activity compared with pure CdIn2S4 octahedron.

The photocatalytic activities of CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-x nanocomposites were also investigated by RhB and TCH solutions degradation under visible light to further evaluate its practical application prospects. Fig. 8 a showed the effect of the adding amount of CQDs in the CQDs/CIS-x nanocomposites on the degradation of RhB. It was obvious that pure CdIn2S4 presented a relatively lower RhB photogradation efficiency of 40.86%. Nevertheless, CQDs/CIS-x nanocomposites obviously exhibited higher photodegradation activities. The photodegradation efficiency was 86.21, 98.33 and 83.10% for CQDs/CIS-1, CQDs/CIS-3 and CQDs/CIS-5, respectively. Fig. 8b displayed the pseudo-first-order dynamic curves of RhB degradation over CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-x nanocomposites. As shown, the degradation rate constants of all CQDs/CIS-x were much higher than that of pure CdIn2S4. The sample CQDs/CIS-3 possessed the largest RhB degradation rate constant of 0.02898 min−1, which was about 8.14-time more than that of CdIn2S4 (0.00356 min−1). In addition, CQDs/CIS-x nanocomposites also presented higher photocatalytic performances for the degradation of TCH (Fig. 8c) than CdIn2S4. Interesting, sample CQDs/CIS-3 still demonstrated the optimal removal ratio of TCH of 82.28% within 120 min. Furthermore, Fig. 8d showed the degradation of TCH over CQDs/CIS-x composite still followed the pseudo-first-order reaction. Obviously, the sample CQDs/CIS-3 also presented the largest TCH degradation rate constant of 0.01312 min−1, which was about 2.32-time as high as that of CdIn2S4 (0.00564 min−1). The larger degradation rate constants for CQDs/CIS-x composites indicated that CQDs/CIS-x composites had superior photocatalytic TCH degradation abilities to CdIn2S4. The enhanced photocatalytic performances of the composite might result from the special morphology of CQDs/CIS-x with novel 3D/2D complex structure, which could efficiently accelerate the separation and transfer of photoinduced electrons not only from the surface of CdIn2S4 to CQDs particles but also from the octahedron to nanosheet. The UV–vis absorbance changes of RhB solutions degraded by CQDs/CIS-3 at different visible light irradiation time are shown at Fig. S3. The main peak at 555 nm corresponding to RhB rapidly decreased with prolonging light irradiation times. Moreover, the position of main peak gradually and slightly shifted to lower wavelength, and finally almost disappeared in 140 min, which indicated the significant and complete destruction of RhB molecules. The TOC measurement of RhB solution degraded by CQDs/CIS-3 was presented in Fig. S4. The TOC removal rate of RhB was 55.8%, suggesting RhB molecules were almost mineralized by the CQDs/CIS-3 sample combined with the result of UV–vis absorbance changes of RhB solutions degraded by CQDs/CIS-3, which was consistent with previous reports (Y. Wang et al., 2021).

The cyclic degradation tests of RhB were further used to evaluate the durability of as-prepared CQDs/CIS-3 for its future practical applications, as shown in Fig. 9 a, CQDs/CIS-3 still presented 95.07% of the first degradation efficiency undergoing fourth cyclic degradation reactions due to the small loss during each cycle. The XRD patterns of CQDs/CIS-3 before and after cycles showed no significant difference, demonstrating the superior stability of CQDs/CIS composite (Fig. 9b).

The capture experiments and ESR technique were carried out to testify the appeared active substances during the photodegradation process (Zhang et al., 2021). In detail, some scavengers such as IPA, TEOA and BQ were applied for capturing hydroxyl radical (·OH), holes (h+) and superoxide radical (·O2 −) (Bavani et al., 2021), respectively. As seen from Fig. 10 a, the degradation efficiency of RhB significantly decreased from 98.33% to 36.9% when BQ was added, indicating the important role of superoxide radical (·O2 −) in the degradation process. And the degradation rate of RhB decreased from 98.33% to 70.83% in the presence of TEOA, which showed that h+ also the primary substance in the photodegradation process and its function was a little smaller than that of h+. When IPA was used, RhB removal of RhB (94.01%) only decreased a little, demonstrating the minor role of hydroxyl radical (·OH) in the photocatalytic process. For further verifying the important function of superoxide radical (·O2 −), the DMPO-·O2 − ESR spectra were performed to analyze the formation and role of ·O2 − in the obtained CdIn2S4 and CQDs/CIS-3. From Fig. 10b, there was no ESR signal corresponding to DMPO-·O2 − adducts in the darkness. When light was on, the typical characteristic peaks belonged to DMPO-·O2 − in pure CIS and CQDs/CIS-3 could be easily detected, suggesting the formation of ·O2 − radicals. Moreover, CQDs/CIS-3 system had the stronger intensity of DMPO-·O2 − signals than in CdIn2S4, indicating more ·O2 − could be produced in the CQDs/CIS heterostructure. ESR result suggested that introduction of CQDs into CdIn2S4 was an efficient strategy to prompt the generation of more active radical ·O2 −, which was beneficial to enhance the photocatalytic activity.

The possible mechanisms for the enhanced photocatalytic performance of CQDs/CIS heterostructure were proposed and presented in Scheme 3 . When CQDs was hybridized into CdIn2S4 system, 3D nanooctahedra/2D nanosheet CQDs/CIS heterostructure could be constructed. When CQDs/CIS hybrid was irradiated under visible light, CdIn2S4 could be excited to produce electrons on the CB and holes on the VB due to its narrow bandgap. In addition, CQDs could convert the visible light into ultraviolet light because of its upconversion luminescence behavior, which could be absorbed by CdIn2S4 to produce more electron-hole pairs, increasing the quantum yield of charge pairs (Shao-Ke et al., 2019). The electrons on CB of CdIn2S4 could be easily trapped by CQDs due to its excellent electron reservoir and the intimate interracial contact between CdIn2S4 and CQDs, limiting the recombination of charges and significantly promoted the migration and separation of electron and hole. For photocatalytic H2 generation, the transferred electrons on CQDs quickly transferred to Pt co-catalyst on the surface of CQDs/CIS hybrid to directly reduce H+ into H2 gas, and the holes in the VB of CdIn2S4 would rapidly react with the sacrificial agent of TEOA. Similarly, for RhB photodegradation, the transferred electrons on the surface of CQDs would readily react with the dissolved O2 to produce ·O2 −, resulting from more negative CB potential of CdIn2S4 (−0.50 eV) than O2/·O2 − potential (−0.046 eV vs. NHE). However, the holes in VB could not oxidize OH− to ·OH due to its more negative EVB than the OH−/·OH potential (+1.99 eV vs. RHE). This result was consistent with those of capture and ESR experiments. Eventually, the produces ·O2 − and long-lived holes could effectively degrade RhB to small molecules.

In conclusion, a series of CQDs/CIS hybrids with 3D/2D mixed structure have been successfully constructed via a facile in-situ solvothermal method. This unique CQDs/CIS hybrid and the effects of CQDs on the morphology evolution of CdIn2S4 crystal growth were first reported. The photocatalytic performances of as-prepared CQDs/CIS hybrids were evaluated by degradation of pollutants (RhB and TCH) under visible-light illustration and H2 generation by solar light photocatalytic water splitting. All the CQDs/CIS hybrid with this unique structure possessed superior photocatalytic activities compared with pure CdIn2S4. The increased surface area, the stronger visible-light absorption ability and expedited transfer and separation of charge pairs in CQDs/CIS hybrid system greatly improved the photocatalytic performances. Furthermore, the CQDs/CIS hybrids also presented superior photostability and recyclability property. This work would provide an important empirical insight for studying the effect of CQDs on the morphology evolution and performance improvement of other CQDs based heterojunction photocatalyst.
